name: ratatouille
instructions:
TWO PARALLEL TRACKS, COMBINED AT THE END:

1. BASE (skillet): sautee yellow onions and bell peppers in olive oil. add canned tomatoes and tomato paste, simmer down into a thick tomato sauce. season with black pepper, fennel powder, fennel seeds (crushed in a mortar), and tarragon.

2. VEGETABLES (separate skillet): sautee eggplant and zucchini SEPARATELY from the base. once cooked through, pat them dry — you want their meat, not their juices. soggy eggplant/zucchini ruins the dish.

3. COMBINE: lay the cooked eggplant and zucchini on top of the tomato sauce at the end. this is the standard ratatouille technique — the vegetables sit on top, they don't simmer in the sauce.
notes:
BOLD CLAIM — FRIED ZUCCHINI + WHITE RICE ARE THE MAIN EVENT, EGGPLANT IS SECONDARY, THE STEW IS A GARNISH: standard ratatouille framing treats the tomato-and-vegetable stew as the base and the fried eggplant/zucchini as toppings — everything roughly co-equal. This recipe rejects that framing. The fried zucchini strips (properly shallow-fried in a lot of olive oil, per the crowding note below) plus the white rice are the actual meal. Eggplant is real but secondary — it plays a supporting texture and vegetable role, not a headliner one. The coarsely-chopped stew of onions, peppers, fennel, and tomato base is a GARNISH — it seasons and adorns the zucchini-and-rice foundation. Consequences of this framing: pay real attention to the zucchini fry (thickness, oil quantity, don't crowd the pan, salt lightly); the rice should be good quality and cooked properly; the stew can be relatively casual and forgiving. If you have to short a component, short the stew, not the fry.

TOMATO FORM — DICED CANNED TOMATOES + TOMATO PASTE, BUT WATCH THE QUANTITY: a can of diced tomatoes (not whole peeled — the diced form breaks down evenly into the soffrito without needing to be crushed) plus a spoon of tomato paste is the canonical tomato source. This works better than raw tomatoes for the concentrated character the dish wants. WANT TO TRY: whole fresh tomatoes someday as a cleaner direction (in want_to_try). NO vinegar — definitely don't add vinegar.

CAUTION AGAINST TOO MUCH TOMATO: the stew is a GARNISH (see bold claim above), not the main event. Overloading with tomato pushes it forward on the plate and swamps the zucchini-and-rice foundation. One can of diced tomatoes plus a modest spoonful of tomato paste is the ceiling — don't dump the whole tube of paste in, don't add a second can. If the sauce feels thin, reduce it further rather than adding more tomato. The concentration should come from cook time, not volume.

ONIONS — SHALLOTS PREFERRED OVER YELLOW ONIONS: shallots are the canonical choice here. Their milder, slightly-sweet character sits under the fennel and tomato without competing; yellow onions read too aggressive and can push the stew forward when it's supposed to be a garnish (see the bold claim above). Yellow onions work in a pinch but shallots are the better move.

BELL PEPPERS: optional. the fennel-bulb + fennel-powder + fennel-seed path tastes more refined and is worth trying as a bell-pepper-free variant. when using bell peppers, julia child recommends green bell peppers over red/yellow/orange — green's more vegetal/grassy character holds up against the tomato better than the sweetness of the colored varieties.

FENNEL SEEDS: best crushed in a mortar. crushed fennel seeds give the dish a nice texture (unlike in a soup, where that texture isn't welcome). fennel powder works too if you don't want to mortar.

GARLIC AND PARSLEY (USE JUDICIOUSLY): the dish sits on an axis between italian and french provincial, and the alliums + herbs are what pull it one way or the other.

- ITALIAN PULL: garlic + parsley + eggplant + tomato paste together pushes the dish into italian / eggplant parmigiana territory.
- FRENCH PULL: bell peppers + anaheim peppers + fennel bulb pull it back toward french provincial.

Match your alliums and herbs to which vegetables you're actually using:

- ZUCCHINI VARIANT (zucchini + raw tomatoes + peppers, NO eggplant): garlic and parsley are welcome. without eggplant and tomato paste, the italian-leaning ingredients aren't there, so adding garlic/parsley doesn't drag the dish away from french.
- EGGPLANT VARIANT (eggplant + tomato paste, NO peppers): skip the garlic. lean on tarragon and extra yellow onion instead. eggplant + tomato paste already pulls italian; adding garlic on top of that crosses the line into vegetable parmigiana.

SALT LIGHTLY — INCREMENTAL, NOT UP-FRONT: an easy failure mode is over-salting or brining the eggplant/zucchini before or during the sauté. Common technique advice is to salt eggplant heavily to draw out bitter water; in this recipe that advice is a trap — the eggplant and zucchini strips get folded on top of an already-seasoned tomato base, and if they're already carrying salt from a brine, the finished dish reads over-salted with no way to walk it back. Better to err on the side of TOO LITTLE salt on the vegetables during their sauté. The tomato base can be salted incrementally as you build it — taste as you go, and adjust once the vegetables land on top. Optimal salt comes from that in-the-loop tasting, not from pre-salting the eggplant.

DON'T OVERCROWD THE EGGPLANT/ZUCCHINI SAUTÉ PAN: even though it's tempting to fit everything in one pan to save time, don't do it. The point of the eggplant/zucchini sauté is to shallow-fry the strips in a generous amount of olive oil so they get real color and their surfaces set — an overcrowded pan turns the sauté into a steam bath instead. The vegetables release water, the oil temperature drops, and you end up with pale, soggy strips (which are the exact failure mode the recipe already warns against). Work in batches. Two batches of properly-fried eggplant beats one crowded batch of steamed-in-oil eggplant every time.

SERVE WITH WHITE RICE: this dish tastes best with white rice rather than bread — unusual for a western dish (the other category that prefers rice over bread is curries). bread soaks up the sauce wrong; rice catches and holds it.
